title: Implement Claim repo with modality + attribution
description: What: Claim rows with modality + attributed_to_type + effective_time + confidence + status. Why: Modality preserves rumor vs assertion distinction. Contract: Rumors don't promote to facts. Verification: A 'rumored' claim stays as candidate.

title: Implement BlockRegistry keyed by kind (shared across surfaces)
description: What: One registry used by chat, Analyze, Home, findings. Why: Consistent rendering across surfaces. Contract: Adding a block kind is a single-registry change. Verification: Same block renders identically in chat + Home.

title: Implement provider bottleneck audit + fallback plan
description: What: Identify slow/rate-limited vendors + fallback strategy. Why: Provider SPOFs are operational risks. Contract: Each provider has at least one fallback path documented. Verification: Inject provider failure; fallback serves.

title: Implement prompt-injection containment test suite
description: What: Adversarial test suite with injection attempts in documents. Why: I4 is too important to be untested. Contract: Suite runs in CI; any analyst exposure fails the build. Verification: Golden set of injection attempts.

title: Implement X/Reddit social ingestion with license gating
description: What: Pipelines for tweets + Reddit posts/threads with license gating. Why: Some social content cannot be stored raw. Contract: license_class routes between blob storage and ephemeral fetch. Verification: Ingest with restrictive license; no blob written.

title: Implement R2/S3 object storage for raw blobs
description: What: Blob storage keyed by content hash. Why: Raw bytes don't belong in relational schema. Contract: Documents reference raw_blob_id; reads are read-only. Verification: Upload blob; fetch by id.

title: Finance Research App — context anchor (vision, invariants, identity rules)
description:
# Finance Research App — context anchor

This bead is a **pinned reference**, not work. It captures the load-bearing design rules that every other bead assumes. When a future session loses context, start here.

## Vision (in one sentence)

> **A finance research app like the one in the reference videos is not "a chatbot with charts." It is an evidence system plus a structured artifact renderer plus a tool-using analyst.**

If you find yourself implementing something that contradicts that sentence (e.g., letting the model emit raw HTML, letting the analyst see untrusted text, letting a displayed number exist without a backing Fact), stop and reconsider.

## Product target

Desktop-first research terminal with five primary workspaces — **Home, Agents, Chat, Screener, Analyze** — plus an entered **Symbol detail** surface family (overview, financials, earnings, holders, signals). The UX expectations lifted from the videos:

- ticker chats and theme/macro chats in the same right-rail thread list
- interactive chart blocks that stay interactive inside old assistant messages
- a structured Analyze memo with editable instructions and source-selection controls
- "Add results to chat"
- a right-rail activity stream showing `Reading` / `Investigating` / `Found` stages
- a Home surface that is **findings-first**, not a raw news page
- light/dark parity; desktop/Electron feel with Expo-friendly later

## Three systems on one data plane

1. **Deterministic terminal surfaces** — watchlists, quotes, charts, symbol tabs, screener, portfolio overlay.
2. **Interactive research chat** — tool-using analyst, structured Block[] responses, persistent threads, cross-thread artifacts.
3. **Background thesis agents** — scheduled research, claim/event monitoring, finding generation, Home feed, right-rail activity, notifications.

These share one evidence plane underneath (facts, claims, events, impacts, snapshots).

## Invariants (I1–I8) — non-negotiable

**I1. No displayed number without a backing row.** Every displayed number must trace to a `Fact` or `Computation` row with provenance, timestamps, and method. The model never invents a number.

**I2. Narrative and visuals cannot disagree.** Prose references facts and claims by ID; values resolve at render time from the same snapshot used by charts and tables.

**I3. Documents are evidence, not truth.** Articles, transcripts, tweets, Reddit threads, uploads are ingested as `Document` objects. They may produce claims/events/candidate-facts. They do not become canonical facts merely by being ingested.

**I4. The analyst never sees raw untrusted text.** Raw external content is processed by the reader path. The analyst consumes only structured outputs. This is the prompt-injection containment boundary.

**I5. Every answer is pinned to a moment.** A response is bound to an immutable `SnapshotManifest`. Interactivity inside the response is allowed only within the snapshot contract.

**I6. Multi-entity reasoning happens through roles and impacts.** A document can mention many subjects, but the reasoning unit is the `Claim` plus its `ClaimArgument`s and `EntityImpact` edges.

**I7. Side effects require approval.** Alerts, exports, emails, private-tool reads, and any transactional tools require explicit user confirmation. Approval-sensitive tools today: `create_alert`, `create_agent`. `add_to_watchlist` is write-intent but can be auto-approved by policy.

**I8. Refresh is explicit.** Interactivity inside a sealed snapshot is allowed only for transforms listed in `allowed_transforms`. Anything requiring fresher data or a changed subject set triggers refresh.

## Identity layer (the most common source of bugs)

- **Issuer** = legal reporting entity (`cik`, `lei`, legal_name).
- **Instrument** = tradable security definition (`isin`, `figi_composite`, `asset_type`, `share_class`).
- **Listing** = venue-specific symbol (`mic`, `ticker`, `trading_currency`, `timezone`).
- **Ticker is a listing locator, not canonical identity.** Same ticker can map to different listings, venues, share classes, ADRs. Never persist or join on ticker where issuer/instrument/listing identity exists.
- `SubjectRef` discriminates: `issuer | instrument | listing | theme | macro_topic | portfolio | screen`.

## Evidence boundary

```
Document  ->  Claim (+ arguments, + impacts)  ->  Event
                 \
                  -> Candidate Fact  ->  (promotion rule)  ->  Fact
```

- Fundamentals consumes `Metric`, `Fact`, `Computation` as the canonical value layer.
- Evidence extraction owns `Source`, `Document`, `Mention`, `Claim`, `ClaimArgument`, `ClaimEvidence`, `ClaimCluster`, `Event`, `EntityImpact`.
- Home feed consumes `Finding`, `SnapshotManifest`, `ClaimCluster` as the deduped product artifact layer.
- Promotion rules by source class:
  - Filing / primary issuer disclosure → may create authoritative facts
  - Earnings transcript → claims and events; facts only via validated extraction
  - News / research notes → claims, events, candidate facts, impacts; rarely authoritative
  - Tweet / X / Reddit → claims, sentiment, leads only
  - User upload → user-scoped evidence unless promoted

## Snapshot contract

```
SnapshotManifest {
  snapshot_id, subject_refs[], as_of, basis, normalization,
  coverage_start, source_ids[], fact_refs[], claim_refs[], event_refs[],
  allowed_transforms: { ranges, intervals, sort_fields, range_end_max: "as_of" }
}
```

- In-snapshot transform: allowed if same subject set, same basis, same normalization, range end ≤ `as_of`, transform listed in `allowed_transforms`.
- Out-of-snapshot: fresher data needed, peer set changes, basis changes, normalization changes, source policy changes → **refresh** (new snapshot).
- This is how historical charts in old messages stay interactive while "answer pinned to a moment" holds.

## Tool architecture (audience boundary)

- **Reader tools** may access raw untrusted text: `search_raw_documents`, `fetch_raw_document`, `extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment`.
- **Analyst tools** operate only on structured outputs. Raw text must not leak.
- The system selects the bundle; the model chooses tools within the bundle.
- Write-intent tools (`create_alert`, `create_agent`, `add_to_watchlist`) remain policy boundaries even when auto-approved.
- Normative registry: `spec/finance_research_tool_registry.json`.

## Block model (assistant output contract)

- Assistant output is always a typed `Block[]`, not markdown and not a tool call.
- Every block inherits `BaseBlock`: `id`, `kind`, `snapshot_id`, `data_ref`, `source_refs[]`, `as_of`.
- Block kinds: `rich_text`, `section`, `metric_row`, `table`, `line_chart`, `revenue_bars`, `perf_comparison`, `segment_donut`, `segment_trajectory`, `metrics_comparison`, `analyst_consensus`, `price_target_range`, `eps_surprise`, `filings_list`, `news_cluster`, `finding_card`, `sentiment_trend`, `mention_volume`, `sources`, `disclosure`.
- `Sources` is required whenever external evidence was used.
- `Disclosure` may be injected by orchestration.
- Chat, Analyze, and agent-produced findings all render through the same `BlockRegistry`.
- Normative schema: `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json`.

## Deliberate non-goals

- live market streaming inside sealed assistant messages
- raw web access by the analyst
- brokerage execution or order management
- implicit trading workflows
- exposing chain-of-thought
- using document mentions as a proxy for entity impact
- treating tweets or Reddit posts as authoritative facts
- making MCP the canonical internal architecture (MCP is optional interop, not the core)

## Normative artifacts

- `stock-agent-v2.md` — consolidated architecture plan
- `spec/finance_research_spec.md` — implementation contract (source of P-codes)
- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` — relational schema pack
- `spec/finance_research_openapi.yaml` — HTTP API contracts
- `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json` — Block[] schema
- `spec/finance_research_tool_registry.json` — tool bundle registry
- `vid1.mp4`, `vid2.mp4` — reference UX

## How to use this graph

1. `bd ready` to see unblocked work.
2. `bd show <id>` for a full briefing on a specific task.
3. `bd dep tree <id>` to see what unblocks when you close something.
4. `bd list --labels phase:P1` to scope to one phase.
5. `bd list --labels surface:chat` to scope to one surface.
6. `bd list --labels invariant:I4` to audit prompt-injection-boundary guardians.

title: Epic P2 — Structured chat & snapshots
description:
# Epic P2 — Structured chat & snapshots

## Goal

Build the interactive research chat: a Durable-Object-style thread coordinator, SSE transport, a pre-resolve router that runs before the analyst, the block registry, the snapshot assembler, and the verifier that enforces I1–I5 at seal time. Assistant output is strictly `Block[]`, never markdown. Every response binds to a sealed `SnapshotManifest`. Old messages remain interactive within the snapshot contract.

## Child tasks

- P2.1 Thread coordinator & SSE transport (session-scoped, per-thread ordering)
- P2.2 Pre-resolve router & budget policy (deterministic, before the analyst loop)
- P2.3 Block registry versioning & initial block catalog
- P2.4 Snapshot assembler & verifier (staging → verify → seal → transform-legality)

## Exit criteria

- Assistant responses validate against `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json` or fail the verifier.
- Every numeric reference in `rich_text` segments resolves to a fact/claim/event id present in the snapshot.
- `/v1/snapshots/{id}/transform` returns 200 for legal in-snapshot transforms and 409 (refresh required) for out-of-snapshot intents.
- Changing a chart range from YTD to 1Y inside an old message works without a refresh, provided the range end ≤ `as_of`.
- SSE events include at minimum: `turn.started`, `tool.started`, `tool.completed`, `snapshot.staged`, `snapshot.sealed`, `block.began`, `block.delta`, `block.completed`, `turn.completed`, `turn.error`.

## Invariants this epic primarily guards

- **I1** (backing row for every number) — verifier rejects responses with orphan refs.
- **I2** (narrative matches visuals) — `rich_text` segments bind fact/claim/event ids resolved from the same snapshot as charts.
- **I5** (answer pinned to a moment) — sealed snapshot manifest is the pin.
- **I8** (explicit refresh) — `allowed_transforms` + 409 boundary.
- **I7** (approvals) — verifier enforces approval gating for pending actions.

## Why this ordering

Without P2 the app is a terminal, not a research product. P2 is also where the prompt-cache ordering (`tools → system → messages`) and structured-output discipline get locked in. P3 (evidence plane) feeds the analyst through PX.2 reader tools, but without the verifier/snapshot plumbing, evidence-backed answers have no place to be pinned. P4 and P5 all render through the same block registry and rely on the snapshot contract.

title: P2.1 Thread coordinator & SSE transport
description:
# P2.1 — Thread coordinator & SSE transport

## What

Stateful per-thread coordinator (Durable-Object-style on Cloudflare, or a process-local actor elsewhere) plus SSE transport for streaming assistant responses. Threads are session-scoped; chat is a primary workspace inside the persistent shell. One coordinator per `thread_id` serializes turn state, tool calls, snapshot staging, and SSE event ordering.

## Why

Serialized per-thread coordination prevents race conditions when tool calls complete out of order, when a user sends a second message mid-stream, or when a verifier rejection must be surfaced alongside a partially-streamed response. Durable Objects are a strong fit because each object is globally addressable, single-threaded, and has strongly-consistent colocated storage.

## Contract / acceptance

- One coordinator per `thread_id`; serializes turn execution.
- SSE event schema: `turn.started`, `tool.started`, `tool.completed`, `snapshot.staged`, `snapshot.sealed`, `block.began`, `block.delta`, `block.completed`, `turn.completed`, `turn.error`.
- Events carry `turn_id`, `block_id` (where relevant), and monotonically increasing sequence numbers.
- Reconnection resumes from the last delivered sequence.
- Chat lives inside the persistent shell (P0.1); the coordinator is session-scoped.
- The coordinator persists `chat_threads.latest_snapshot_id` after sealing.
- `chat_messages` are written after the snapshot is sealed — never before.

## Depends on

- P0.1 (shell — Chat is a primary workspace inside)
- P0.6 (SSE route stub — replaces with real wiring here)
- P2.4 (snapshot sealing — writes happen after sealing)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.3 (block registry — blocks stream via `block.began/delta/completed`)
- PX.3 (frontend renderer — consumes SSE and drives UI updates)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Cloudflare Durable Objects have a single-thread execution model — use it deliberately for serialization but don't starve concurrent turns for different threads.
- Don't write `chat_messages` until `snapshot.sealed` — persisting before verifier approval means an invariant-violating message becomes history.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/chat/ThreadCoordinator.ts` (Durable Object or actor equivalent)
- `services/chat/SSE.ts`
- `/v1/chat/threads/*` handlers

title: Implement /v1/chat/threads CRUD (list, create, archive, title update)
description: What: Thread CRUD surface per OpenAPI. Why: Chat workspace needs thread list; not part of turn coordinator. Contract: List by user + updated_at desc; create with optional primary_subject_ref; archive via soft-delete. Verification: End-to-end: create thread, list, archive.

title: P2.2 Pre-resolve router & budget policy
description:
# P2.2 — Pre-resolve router & budget policy

## What

A deterministic orchestration stage that runs *before* the analyst tool loop: extracts subject candidates, resolves the period, loads the thread summary and prior snapshot, detects trivial fast paths, enforces auth + rate limits, selects the tool bundle, and sets the turn's cost budget. The analyst never sees raw user text without first going through this stage.

## Why

Deterministic pre-resolve consumes resolver envelopes rather than asking the model to silently choose among ambiguous identity candidates. It also keeps token costs bounded: distinguishing lightweight quote reads from heavier historical-bar/fundamentals/evidence reads lets the budget policy pick the right cost class without guessing from provider names or ticker text.

## Contract / acceptance

- Inputs: user message, thread summary, prior snapshot manifest (if any), auth context.
- Stages: subject extraction → period resolution → fast-path detection → auth/rate gate → bundle selection → budget setting.
- Subject extraction uses P0.3 resolver; ambiguity is preserved (not silently resolved).
- Bundle selection: the **system** chooses the bundle; the **model** chooses tools within the bundle.
- Bundles (from `finance_research_tool_registry.json`): `quote_lookup`, `single_subject_analysis`, `peer_comparison`, `theme_research`, `segment_deep_dive`, `document_research`, `filing_research`, `screener`, `agent_management`, `alert_management`, `analyze_template_run`.
- Budget policy has per-cost-class caps (low/medium/high tool calls) and a global per-turn cap.
- Prompt-cache ordering is respected: the prefix is `tools → system → bundle policy → response schema → few-shots → thread summary → resolved subjects/period`; volatile user turn is last.

## Depends on

- P0.3 (resolver)
- P0.3b (search-to-subject flow)
- P1.1 (quote cost class)
- P1.2 (fundamentals cost class)
- PX.2 (tool runtime + registry)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.1 (thread coordinator invokes this before the analyst loop)
- P5.2 (agent runs reuse the same bundle-selection discipline)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Never let raw user text bypass this stage into tool calls — prompt-injection containment starts here.
- Prompt-cache TTL is 5 minutes (per Anthropic caching docs). Building the cacheable prefix correctly materially affects cost.
- Fast-path detection should produce a deterministic response (e.g., a quote-only turn) without invoking the analyst at all.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/chat/PreResolve.ts`
- `services/chat/BundleSelector.ts`
- `services/chat/BudgetPolicy.ts`

title: P2.3 Block registry versioning & initial block catalog
description:
# P2.3 — Block registry versioning & initial block catalog

## What

The `BlockRegistry`: a typed registry keyed by block `kind` with a JSON-schema validator wired to `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json` and a renderer per block kind. Assistant output is **always** `Block[]`, not markdown and not tool calls. Chat, Analyze, and agent-produced findings render through the same registry.

## Why

A shared registry is how "generative UI" stays coherent: rich_text in chat, a Finding card on Home, and an Analyze memo section all use the same React component instance with the same data_ref/snapshot_id contract. Without the registry, each surface invents its own renderer and the snapshot contract fragments.

## Contract / acceptance

- Every block validates against `spec/finance_research_block_schema.json`.
- BaseBlock fields are universal: `id`, `kind`, `snapshot_id`, `data_ref`, `source_refs[]`, `as_of`, optional `title`, `disclosure_tier`, `interactive`.
- Canonical schema field is `data_ref` (NOT `dataRef` or `queryRef`).
- Block kinds in the initial catalog: `rich_text`, `section`, `metric_row`, `table`, `line_chart`, `revenue_bars`, `perf_comparison`, `segment_donut`, `segment_trajectory`, `metrics_comparison`, `analyst_consensus`, `price_target_range`, `eps_surprise`, `filings_list`, `news_cluster`, `finding_card`, `sentiment_trend`, `mention_volume`, `sources`, `disclosure`.
- `RichText` binds facts/claims/events by reference: segments are `{type: "text", text}` or `{type: "ref", ref_kind, ref_id, format?}`.
- `Section` is first-class and holds child blocks.
- `Sources` is required whenever external evidence was used.
- `Disclosure` may be injected by orchestration.
- Registry versioning: adding a block kind is a backward-compatible change; renaming/removing requires a migration.
- Chat, Analyze, and findings render via the same registry keyed by `kind`.

## Depends on

- P1.1b (stable series query identity + in-snapshot cache rules — so interactive blocks know which transforms are legal)
- P2.4 (snapshot rules — registry needs to know what's sealed)

## Downstream consumers

- PX.3 (frontend renderer uses the registry)
- P4.2 (Analyze memos render via this)
- P4.4 (Home feed renders finding_card via this)
- P5.3 (agent findings render summary_blocks via this)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT let the model emit HTML or markdown. Any "markdown-ish" block is rendered client-side from typed rich_text segments.
- RichText ref resolution is client-side: the renderer looks up fact/claim/event ids in the snapshot manifest.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/blocks/Registry.ts`
- `app/blocks/<Kind>.tsx` (one per kind)
- `services/chat/BlockValidator.ts` (JSON-schema wire)

title: Implement Narrative + layout block renderers (rich_text, section, metric_row, table)
description: What: React renderers for the 4 narrative/layout block kinds. Why: Narrative is the core of Block[] and serves all surfaces. Contract: rich_text renders text + ref segments; section is collapsible container; metric_row is horizontal metric chips; table is static data grid. Verification: Each block validates + renders correctly for sample inputs.

title: Implement Chart + comparison block renderers (line_chart, revenue_bars, perf_comparison, segment_donut, segment_trajectory, metrics_comparison, sentiment_trend, mention_volume)
description: What: React renderers for the 8 chart/comparison block kinds. Why: Charts are the interactive surface in old messages (snapshot-safe transforms). Contract: All chart renderers fetch data via snapshot_id + transform endpoint; respect allowed_transforms for in-place range changes. Verification: Render each block with fixtures + run an in-snapshot transform on one.

title: Implement Research + evidence block renderers (analyst_consensus, price_target_range, eps_surprise, filings_list, news_cluster, finding_card)
description: What: React renderers for the 6 research/evidence summary block kinds. Why: These are the 'finding-style' blocks consumed by chat, Analyze memos, and Home. Contract: finding_card uses sealed snapshot for replay; news_cluster binds to ClaimCluster dedupe. Verification: Render each block + verify ref resolution.

title: Implement Trust + provenance block renderers (sources, disclosure)
description: What: React renderers for the 2 trust blocks. Why: Sources is the required provenance surface; Disclosure is the trust/compliance surface orchestration may inject. Contract: sources lists referenced documents with trust_tier badges; disclosure renders plain text items with tier indicator. Verification: Chat turn with external evidence auto-renders Sources block.

title: P2.4 Snapshot assembler & verifier
description:
# P2.4 — Snapshot assembler & verifier

## What

The deterministic stage that runs after the analyst tool loop and before streaming the response: stage the snapshot manifest (collect fact/claim/event/series/source/tool-call refs from the tool log), verify bindings and disclosures, seal the snapshot transactionally, and enforce `allowed_transforms` on subsequent interactions.

## Why

This is where invariants I1 (no displayed number without a backing row), I2 (narrative matches visuals), I5 (answer pinned to a moment), I7 (approvals), and I8 (explicit refresh) are physically enforced. Without a verifier, the model can return block shapes that pass schema validation but reference nonexistent facts — producing confident fabrication.

## Contract / acceptance

**Staging:**
- Assemble `SnapshotManifest` from tool-call logs: `subject_refs`, `fact_refs`, `claim_refs`, `event_refs`, `series_specs`, `source_ids`, `tool_call_ids`, `as_of`, `basis`, `normalization`, `coverage_start`.
- Compute `allowed_transforms` explicitly (ranges, intervals, sort fields, `range_end_max: as_of`).

**Verifier checks (all must pass):**
- Every `data_ref` in blocks resolves to a staged fact/series/bundle.
- Every RichText `ref_segment` resolves to a fact/claim/event id in the manifest.
- All cited `source_refs[]` exist in staged `source_ids`.
- Numeric refs match format/period/unit consistency.
- Required disclosures are present (delayed pricing, filing-time basis, low analyst coverage, candidate vs authoritative, etc.).
- All side-effecting tool invocations have approval states resolved (pending or approved) — nothing executes directly from model output.

**Sealing:**
- Snapshot ID minted atomically with manifest persistence.
- `chat_messages.snapshot_id` written only after successful seal.
- Failed verification writes `verifier_fail_logs` row and fails the turn gracefully.

**Transform enforcement:**
- `POST /v1/snapshots/{id}/transform` with a transform listed in `allowed_transforms` and `range_end <= as_of` → 200 with transformed data.
- Transform requiring fresher data, peer-set change, basis change, or normalization change → 409 with `refresh_required`.
- Clients must not mutate sealed snapshots in any path.

## Depends on

- P2.3 (block registry — knows what to verify)
- PX.2 (tool runtime — provides tool call logs + approval state)
- P1.1b (series identity — what's legal as an in-snapshot transform)

## Downstream consumers

- P2.1 (thread coordinator gates message persistence on seal success)
- P4.3 (shared artifact flow — reuses sealed snapshots)
- PX.3 (frontend renderer — enforces presentation-only interactions inside snapshot scope)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Seal order matters: verify → seal → persist chat_messages → stream block.completed events. Streaming before sealing risks showing an invariant-violating block.
- `allowed_transforms` is explicit manifest state, not a UI guess.
- Transforms are SNAPSHOT-level, not block-level. Block kind alone doesn't determine legality.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/snapshot/Assembler.ts`
- `services/snapshot/Verifier.ts`
- `services/snapshot/Sealer.ts`
- `/v1/snapshots/*` handlers

title: Implement disclosure policy compiler
description: What: Compiler that derives required disclosure blocks from snapshot state (delayed pricing, filing-time basis, low-coverage, candidate-vs-authoritative, FX-converted values). Why: Verifier checks disclosures present; something must compile them. Contract: Compiler is deterministic given snapshot manifest. Verification: Snapshot with delayed quote always produces delayed-pricing disclosure.

title: Implement Theme + ThemeMembership models
description: What: Theme table + membership table with manual/rule_based/inferred modes. Why: Themes are first-class subjects. Contract: SubjectRef.kind='theme' works end-to-end. Verification: Create theme; add members; resolve.

title: Implement golden-eval runner + result persistence
description: What: Nightly eval runner writes eval_run_results. Why: Catch drift. Contract: Golden set covers the spec's 14 categories. Verification: Runner produces results; comparison highlights drift.

title: Implement virtualized message list + memoized blocks
description: What: Windowing for long threads; blocks memoized by content hash. Why: Performance at scale. Contract: 1000-message thread scrolls at 60fps. Verification: Perf benchmark.

title: Implement severity scorer (evidence × impact × relevance)
description: What: Composite score mapped to low/medium/high/critical. Why: Ranking + alert gating. Contract: Score is explainable. Verification: Golden inputs produce expected severities.

title: Implement Mention prominence classifier (headline/lead/body/incidental)
description: What: Classify prominence per mention. Why: Home-feed ranking depends on prominence. Contract: Headline mentions weigh more than body mentions. Verification: Same subject in headline vs footnote gets different prominence.

title: Implement deep-links to symbol detail / theme / Analyze
description: What: Card click navigates to the right deterministic surface. Why: No custom-readout-per-card. Contract: Clicks route via canonical SubjectRef. Verification: Click card about AAPL earnings; lands on /symbol/:aaplRef/earnings.

title: Implement scroll tailing + jump-to-latest affordance
description: What: Auto-tail new messages; jump button when user scrolled up. Why: Classic chat UX. Contract: Tailing respects user scroll position. Verification: Scroll up; new message appears; jump button shows.

title: Implement ClaimArgument role model
description: What: ClaimArgument rows binding claim to subjects with role. Why: Multi-entity reasoning lives here. Contract: Roles are subject/object/customer/supplier/competitor/regulator/beneficiary/constrained_party/affected_party. Verification: A supply-chain claim produces arguments for all affected parties.

title: Implement Source repo + trust_tier + license_class
description: What: Repo for sources with trust tier and license class. Why: Promotion rules key off trust tier; licensing gates raw storage. Contract: Source row required before any Document references it. Verification: Orphan document creation fails.

title: Implement queue message handler with idempotency
description: What: Handler detects duplicate run_id and short-circuits. Why: Queues are at-least-once. Contract: No side effects on duplicate. Verification: Replay same message; no duplicate finding.

title: Implement refresh boundary detection and error envelope
description: What: Requests requiring fresher data, different peers, basis, normalization return 409 with refresh_required envelope. Why: Silent mutation of sealed snapshot violates I5. Contract: 409 payload names the dimension that triggered refresh. Verification: Changing peer set yields 409 with peer_set as reason.

title: Implement per-agent concurrency cap
description: What: Only one run per agent at a time. Why: Overlapping runs race on watermarks. Contract: Cap default 1; configurable up to small N. Verification: Burst-fire messages; sequenced execution.

title: Implement Home ranking (recency + severity + user affinity)
description: What: Weighted ranking with configurable weights. Why: Volume without severity weighting drowns critical findings. Contract: Critical severity always surfaces; recency decays. Verification: Ranking test suite with known inputs.

title: Epic P0 — Foundation & shell
description:
# Epic P0 — Foundation & shell

## Goal

Stand up the durable scaffolding everything else plugs into: the persistent workspace shell, auth session model, route skeleton, identity resolver service, symbol search, first quote snapshot, manual watchlist baseline, relational schema (from `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql`), and the observability skeleton. At the end of P0 a user can open the app (authenticated or not), search for a ticker, land on a subject-detail shell showing a live quote, and (if authenticated) save it to a default watchlist.

## Child tasks

- P0.1 App shell & auth session/gating
- P0.2 Route skeleton & right-rail slot
- P0.3 Identity & resolver service
- P0.3b Search-to-subject resolution flow
- P0.4 Symbol search & first quote snapshot surface
- P0.4b Manual watchlist management baseline
- P0.5 DB bootstrap (apply schema pack + migrations)
- P0.6 Dev infra & observability skeleton

## Exit criteria

- `bd ready` from P1 tasks becomes populated (i.e., their P0 blockers are closed).
- User can navigate Home / Screener / Analyze-entry / Symbol-detail without a session.
- User can search "AAPL", pick among resolver candidates, and land on the subject-detail shell with a freshness-stamped quote.
- Authenticated user can add that subject to the default manual watchlist.
- `/v1/subjects/resolve` returns `resolved | ambiguous | not_found` envelopes.
- Database has applied `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` and tables are populated with reference data.

## Invariants this epic primarily guards

- **I5** (answers pinned to a moment) is seeded here via the listing-oriented quote snapshot with `as_of`.
- Canonical identity discipline (issuer vs instrument vs listing; ticker is a locator).

## Why this ordering

Everything downstream assumes the resolver returns canonical `SubjectRef`s rather than raw ticker strings, that the shell owns navigation and right-rail slots (so P4.5 activity and P2.1 chat can plug in), and that the schema is applied (so P1.* services have tables to query). Skipping any P0 piece forces shortcuts that become invariant violations later (e.g., P1.1 calling a provider with a ticker string instead of a listing id).

title: P0.1 App shell & auth session/gating
description:
# P0.1 — App shell & auth session/gating

## What

One persistent workspace shell that owns three regions: left navigation, main canvas, right-rail slot. Auth is *in-shell*: unauthenticated users may browse public routes (Home, Screener, Analyze-entry, symbol detail). Protected routes (Chat, Agents, watchlists) render an in-shell auth gate instead of redirecting to a separate auth page.

## Why

The shell is the durable navigation frame regardless of auth state. Putting auth on the shell instead of the routes means session loss degrades gracefully (protected panels re-gate in place; public content keeps rendering). This contract is why Chat can live in the same shell as public Home without Home becoming session-scoped.

## Contract / acceptance

- Left navigation contains `Home`, `Agents`, `Chat`, `Screener`, `Analyze`.
- Shell chrome persists across all primary-workspace transitions.
- Unauthenticated users can enter the shell and navigate to public surfaces.
- Protected workspaces (`Chat`, `Agents`, user-owned watchlists, persisted Analyze runs) use soft in-shell guards; they do not redirect.
- Public routes may launch protected actions via inline auth interrupts (e.g., `save to watchlist`) that preserve the current route + pending-action payload so sign-in resumes the action.
- Session expiry inside a protected surface collapses protected content to the in-shell auth gate; public surfaces keep rendering.
- The shell is *not* auth-gated as a whole.

## Depends on

- None (foundation work).

## Downstream consumers

- P0.2 Route skeleton (lives inside this shell).
- P0.4 Symbol search (uses shell-owned search entry).
- P2.1 Thread coordinator (Chat is a primary workspace in this shell).
- P4.5 Right-rail activity (uses shell-owned right-rail slot).

## Considerations / gotchas

- Auth backend is intentionally abstract here — the contract is written in terms of "authenticated session scope" rather than a specific identity provider. Don't over-specify the identity backend in this bead.
- The right-rail slot is *shell-owned*, not surface-owned. Screener defaults to a denser main-canvas layout without using the rail; it may opt in later without changing the shell contract.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/shell/WorkspaceShell.tsx` (or framework equivalent)
- `app/shell/AuthGate.tsx`
- `app/shell/RightRailSlot.tsx`
- Auth session middleware / route guard

title: Implement persistent workspace shell with three regions
description: What: Left nav, main canvas, right-rail slot in one component that persists across primary-workspace transitions. Why: The shell is the durable navigation frame regardless of auth; surface swaps happen inside it. Contract: Shell chrome never unmounts on workspace transitions. Verification: Navigating Home -> Chat -> Screener keeps nav selection state and does not flash the shell.
close_reason: Shell with three persistent regions implemented; invariant verified via Playwright (nav tag survived /home -> /chat -> /screener); build + typecheck pass. See commit 4cea911.

title: Implement in-shell auth gate (soft guards, not redirects)
description: What: Protected workspaces render an auth gate in the main canvas instead of redirecting. Why: Redirects break the persistent-shell contract and lose return-to context. Contract: Session loss inside a protected surface collapses content to the auth gate; public surfaces keep rendering. Verification: Log out while on /chat/:id — Chat pane gates, Home is unaffected.
close_reason: Implemented in-shell auth gate (soft guards, no redirects). Added AuthProvider (mock identity-agnostic session), ProtectedSurface guard, AuthGate main-canvas UI, SessionControl affordance. Chat and Agents wrapped with ProtectedSurface. Verified in browser: unauthenticated /chat entry preserves URL and shell chrome while collapsing main canvas to the gate; sign-out while on /chat collapses Chat in place; Home keeps rendering throughout. Lint and build green.

title: Implement inline auth interrupt (preserves route + pending action)
description: What: Public routes launching protected actions preserve the current route and pending-action payload so sign-in resumes the action. Why: Videos show save-to-watchlist from public pages; forcing route change breaks flow. Contract: After sign-in the user returns to the same public route and the pending action completes. Verification: Unauth click 'Add to watchlist' -> sign in -> add completes without leaving the page.
close_reason: Implemented inline auth interrupt. AuthInterruptProvider owns pending action slot; session null→truthy transition flushes pending via useEffect (so modal Sign-in + any future programmatic session restore both resume the action). AuthInterrupt modal mounted in shell. Demo consumer on HomePage (Save AAPL to watchlist) verifies all four paths: unauthed→modal→signin→action fires, authed→immediate fire, cancel→discard, URL never changes. Lint and build green.

title: Implement light/dark mode parity with theme toggle
description: What: Theme tokens + toggle wired across the persistent shell and all surfaces. Why: Spec calls out light/dark parity as a first-class UX requirement visible in videos. Contract: All shell + surface components respect theme tokens; toggle persists per user. Verification: Toggle theme in one surface; all surfaces update without remount.

title: P0.2 Route skeleton & right-rail slot
description:
# P0.2 — Route skeleton & right-rail slot

## What

Nested-route structure matching the spec's route model: primary workspace routes (`/home`, `/agents`, `/chat/:threadId`, `/screener`, `/analyze`) plus an entered subject-detail shell (`/symbol/:subjectRef/{overview|financials|earnings|holders|signals}`). These are app navigation route groups, *not* `/v1/*` HTTP endpoint groups.

## Why

Nested routes are the durable model for subject-detail sections because the subject-detail shell must own shared subject header context and local section navigation while the outer workspace shell persists. Conflating symbol routes into a top-level workspace breaks the "entered subject" model the videos show.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Chat` is thread-scoped, not symbol-scoped (threads may span themes, multiple subjects, or imported Analyze artifacts).
- `Analyze` is a top-level workspace (not only a symbol-detail tab), but it accepts deep-linked `SubjectRef` context from symbol detail.
- `Screener` remains a primary workspace for building + viewing one active screen plus results.
- Symbol detail is an entered route group keyed by canonical subject identity — entering swaps the main canvas into a subject-detail shell while preserving outer shell chrome.
- Initial durable subject-detail sections: `overview`, `financials`, `earnings`, `holders`, `signals`.
- `signals` is the extensible section for community, sentiment, news pulse, future alt-data — not a source-specific `/reddit` route.

## Depends on

- P0.1 (app shell must exist before routes can live inside it).

## Downstream consumers

- P0.4, P1.3, P1.3b, P1.4b, P2.1, P4.2, P4.4, P4.5 all depend on this route skeleton.

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do not add a `/symbol/:ticker/*` route. Use the canonical `SubjectRef` identity so dual-class, ADR, and cross-listing cases work.
- `Analyze` accepting deep-linked subject context is a *workspace transition*, not a symbol-detail tab expansion.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/routes/*` (framework router config)
- `app/shell/SubjectDetailShell.tsx`
notes: When adding /chat/:id and other nested protected routes, wrap each protected route element with <ProtectedSurface destinationLabel="..."> (web/src/shell/ProtectedSurface.tsx). Route-element guards set in fra-6al.1.2 do NOT cascade to child route configs — each nested protected route needs its own wrap. Today only /chat and /agents are wrapped.

title: Implement primary workspace routes (/home, /agents, /chat, /screener, /analyze)
description: What: Route groups inside the shell. Chat is thread-scoped (/chat/:threadId). Why: These are app navigation routes, not /v1/* endpoint groups. Contract: All five primary routes render inside the same shell. Verification: URL changes on nav; shell chrome does not remount.

title: Implement entered subject-detail shell with nested sections
description: What: /symbol/:subjectRef/{overview|financials|earnings|holders|signals} — main canvas swaps into a subject-detail shell while outer shell persists. Why: Subject sections are durable nested routes, not dynamic tabs. Contract: The subject-detail shell owns shared subject header context and local section navigation. Verification: Overview -> financials preserves subject context and shell chrome.

title: Implement session-scope route metadata (public vs protected)
description: What: Route table carries public/protected flags consumed by the in-shell auth gate. Why: Route-level metadata is how the gate knows when to show. Contract: Public: Home, Screener, Analyze-entry, entered symbol-detail. Protected: Chat, Agents, user-owned watchlists, persisted Analyze runs. Verification: Unauthed /home renders; unauthed /chat renders auth gate.

title: P0.3 Identity & resolver service
description:
# P0.3 — Identity & resolver service

## What

The deterministic boundary that converts lookup input (user text or provider-origin identity records) into canonical finance identity outputs: `issuer`, `instrument`, `listing`, or a typed `SubjectRef`. The resolver owns normalization, candidate generation, canonical selection when unambiguous, and explicit ambiguity preservation when multiple canonical targets remain plausible.

## Why

Ticker is a listing locator, not canonical identity. The same symbol can map to different listings, venues, share classes, or ADRs. Without the resolver as a hard boundary, the rest of the system accumulates ticker-string shortcuts that break on dual-class stocks, cross-listings, and restructurings.

## Contract / acceptance

Resolution envelope has three outcomes:

- `resolved` — one canonical target confidently enough for deterministic downstream use.
- `ambiguous` — multiple canonical issuer/instrument/listing/SubjectRef targets remain plausible; returns ranked candidates without silently picking one.
- `not_found` — input normalizable but not mappable to a supported canonical target.

Rules:

- Input families: user-entered lookup text (ticker-like strings, issuer names, aliases) and provider-origin identity records (ticker, exchange, CIK, ISIN, other id-bearing fields).
- Normalization must not erase identity-level distinctions between issuer, instrument, and listing.
- The resolver may start from ticker/alias lookup but must promote the lookup into explicit issuer/instrument/listing/SubjectRef output.
- Ranked candidates are advisory metadata, not permission to silently collapse ambiguity.
- `resolved` responses include the canonical identity level that was chosen.

## Depends on

- P0.5 DB bootstrap (tables `issuers`, `instruments`, `listings`, `themes`).

## Downstream consumers

- P0.3b (search-to-subject flow)
- P1.1 (market data) — listing-appropriate subject handoff, never ticker string
- P1.2 (fundamentals) — issuer-appropriate subject handoff
- P2.2 (pre-resolve router) — consumes resolver envelopes
- P4.1 (themes) and all screener/watchlist/portfolio handoffs

## Considerations / gotchas

- Maintain the three outcomes (`resolved | ambiguous | not_found`) as a strict contract. Silent winner-picking here corrupts every downstream invariant.
- Ambiguity axis matters: "issuer-vs-listing" and "multiple listings for one ticker" are different ambiguity modes that the API should surface when detectable.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/resolver/*`
- `/v1/subjects/resolve` handler
- `/v1/subjects/hydrate` handler
- DB indexes: `issuers_cik_idx`, `issuers_lei_idx`, `instruments_isin_idx`, `listings_ticker_idx`

title: Implement resolver envelope (resolved / ambiguous / not_found)
description: What: Typed response envelope the resolver returns with three outcomes — resolved, ambiguous, not_found. Why: Ambiguity-preserving contracts are the only way to avoid silent winner-picking. Contract: Each resolution returns exactly one of three outcomes with ranked candidates when ambiguous. Verification: 'GOOG' returns ranked candidates; 'NOTREAL' returns not_found; 'AAPL' returns resolved with canonical listing.

title: Implement normalization without identity-level collapse
description: What: Normalizer preserves issuer-vs-instrument-vs-listing distinctions while matching input tokens. Why: Collapsing GOOG/GOOGL or ADR classes hides invariants downstream. Contract: Normalized form never merges rows representing different canonical identities. Verification: 'google' returns separate candidates for issuer vs GOOG vs GOOGL.

title: Implement ticker/alias/CIK/ISIN entry points promoting to SubjectRef
description: What: Accept multiple input families but always output canonical SubjectRef. Why: Provider payloads arrive with varied id fields. Contract: resolver(ticker='AAPL'), resolver(cik='0000320193'), resolver(isin='US0378331005') all arrive at the same canonical Apple identity. Verification: Tests assert cross-id-family equivalence.

title: Wire /v1/subjects/resolve handler per OpenAPI
description: What: HTTP endpoint matching finance_research_openapi.yaml. Why: Frontend only talks to /v1/*; no direct client-to-provider calls. Contract: POST /v1/subjects/resolve accepts {text, allow_kinds?} returns envelope. Verification: Contract test against OpenAPI schema.

title: P0.3b Search-to-subject resolution flow
description:
# P0.3b — Search-to-subject resolution flow

## What

The deterministic orchestration that carries lookup input from candidate search through canonical selection into downstream-safe subject hydration: **candidate search → canonical selection → hydrated subject handoff**.

## Why

Candidate search may return zero, one, or many candidates. Without a disciplined flow, downstream consumers invent their own winner-picking, producing identity drift. The hydrated bundle is the single shape watchlists, chat, Analyze entry, and service calls all consume.

## Contract / acceptance

- Unique deterministic candidate satisfying the resolver contract may auto-advance without a separate chooser step.
- Multiple plausible candidates pause at explicit ambiguity preservation and surface ranked candidates for later user/caller choice.
- `not_found` ends the flow without subject hydration.
- Only `resolved` produces hydrated subject handoff.
- Hydrated subject bundle contains: canonical `SubjectRef`, resolved identity level, stable display labels, normalized lookup input, resolution path (`auto_advanced` | `explicit_choice`), sufficient issuer/instrument/listing context for immediate downstream use.
- Downstream systems persist only the canonical `SubjectRef`; other bundle fields are entry context that may be refreshed later.

## Depends on

- P0.3 (resolver service)

## Downstream consumers

- P0.4 (symbol search surface)
- P1.1 (market data quote/bar retrieval)
- P1.2 (fundamentals statement normalization)
- P2.2 (pre-resolve router in chat turn loop)
- Watchlist + saved-subject entry flows

## Considerations / gotchas

- Resolution path (`auto_advanced` vs `explicit_choice`) is useful telemetry — log it via PX.1.
- One hydrated bundle may carry joined issuer + active listing context. The canonical `SubjectRef` remains the durable key.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/resolver/flow.ts`
- `app/search/SearchToSubject.tsx`
- `/v1/subjects/resolve` (handler wiring)

title: Implement candidate search -> canonical selection -> hydrated handoff flow
description: What: Three-stage pipeline with auto-advance and explicit-choice paths. Why: Downstream consumers should never reconstruct this. Contract: Unique deterministic hit auto-advances; multiple plausible hits pause at ambiguity; not_found ends. Verification: Flow tests for all three branches.

title: Implement hydrated subject bundle contract
description: What: Bundle shape carries canonical SubjectRef, identity level, display labels, normalized input, resolution path, and issuer/instrument/listing context. Why: Single shape every subject consumer consumes. Contract: Persisted key is only the SubjectRef; other fields are entry context. Verification: Watchlists, Chat composer, Analyze entry all consume the same shape.

title: Log resolution path (auto_advanced / explicit_choice) to PX.1
description: What: Emit telemetry distinguishing auto-advance from explicit-choice resolutions. Why: Useful signal for resolver quality and UX friction measurement. Contract: Every resolution writes to tool_call_logs with the path. Verification: Query logs by path and see both branches populated.

title: P0.4 Symbol search & first quote snapshot surface
description:
# P0.4 — Symbol search & first quote snapshot surface

## What

Shell-owned symbol search entry that, on successful resolution, enters symbol detail with a minimum quote snapshot landing state. The snapshot is the initial landing state of entered symbol detail — not a competing top-level workspace or a detached quote page.

## Why

The videos show ticker entry flows that immediately land on a subject page showing price, move, session state, and a small recent chart. That experience depends on the shell owning search so downstream surfaces (watchlists, portfolio, chat composer) reuse the same search contract instead of redefining symbol entry per surface.

## Contract / acceptance

- Shell owns the primary symbol search entry.
- Unique deterministic hits auto-advance; ambiguous hits require explicit choice; `not_found` ends without subject hydration.
- Successful subject resolution enters symbol detail (main canvas swaps into subject-detail shell while outer workspace shell persists).
- Minimum quote snapshot landing content: canonical subject display identity, listing-sensitive trading symbol context, latest price, absolute move, percentage move, freshness/session state, small recent-range or chart hook.
- Quote retrieval is listing-oriented even when the hydrated subject also carries issuer context.
- A light issuer summary/profile companion is allowed when available but is best-effort and must not block landing state.
- This bead does not define earnings, holders, filings, peer tables, or a finished overview surface.

## Depends on

- P0.3b (search-to-subject flow)
- P1.1 (quote retrieval contract) — note: the snapshot surface is defined here but the underlying quote call lives in P1.1; until P1.1 exists, this surface can stub.

## Downstream consumers

- P0.4b (manual watchlist — reuses search entry + selected-subject handoff)
- P1.3 (symbol overview shell) — extends the thin landing state into a durable core tab
- P1.5 (portfolio/watchlist basics) — reuse quote-row hydration shape

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do not embed provider-specific fetch logic here. Quote comes from P1.1's provider-neutral contract.
- The landing state is intentionally thin — resist the urge to add earnings, holders, or peer modules. That's P1.3.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/shell/SymbolSearch.tsx`
- `app/symbol/QuoteSnapshot.tsx`
- `app/shell/SubjectDetailShell.tsx` (landing slot)

title: Implement shell-owned symbol search entry
description: What: The one symbol search input every surface reuses. Why: Per-surface search reimplementation is how ticker-string shortcuts creep in. Contract: All surfaces target the same component and the same /v1/subjects/resolve call. Verification: Add-to-watchlist and shell header use the same component.

title: Implement subject-detail shell landing with minimum quote snapshot
description: What: Market identity strip + price-first quote snapshot (price, absolute move, pct, freshness, small chart hook). Why: Immediate landing UX shown in videos. Contract: Quote retrieval is listing-oriented; issuer profile is best-effort non-blocking. Verification: Entering symbol detail shows the landing within one render cycle.

title: Wire quote hydration via P1.1 stub
description: What: Temporary stub for P1.1 market-data call so landing renders end-to-end during P0. Why: Unblocks P0.4b and P1.3 work without waiting for full market-data service. Contract: Stub returns fixture with correct shape (as_of, delay_class, currency, source_id). Verification: Replacing stub with P1.1 requires no UI change.

title: Implement symbol typeahead/autocomplete
description: What: Debounced typeahead that queries /v1/subjects/resolve and surfaces ranked candidates inline. Why: Videos show fluid symbol entry; forcing Enter before results feels slow. Contract: Typeahead reuses shell-owned search component; up/down arrow selection; Enter auto-advances on unique result. Verification: Type 'AAP' — see ranked candidates within 200ms; select with arrows.

title: P0.4b Manual watchlist management baseline
description:
# P0.4b — Manual watchlist management baseline

## What

One implicit default manual watchlist as the baseline saved-subject model, with membership-only CRUD: view current members, add a resolved subject, remove a saved subject. Membership is canonical subject identity, not ticker strings.

## Why

Watchlists are the foundation of every saved-universe concept: dynamic watchlists (P4.7), agent universes (P5.1), screens (P1.4), themes (P4.1), and portfolio overlays (P1.5b) all build on top of a simple, user-owned collection. Getting the baseline membership contract right here prevents a sprawl of subject-collection systems later.

## Contract / acceptance

- Baseline CRUD is membership-only: view, add, remove.
- This bead does not define create-list, rename-list, delete-list, sharing, reordering, or multiple manual lists.
- The persisted membership unit is canonical subject identity — never a raw ticker string or stored quote payload.
- Membership is idempotent at subject level (adding the same canonical subject twice does not create duplicates).
- Manual watchlist rows hydrate quote context on read from the saved subject identity.
- Row hydration contract is lightweight: subject display identity, listing-sensitive symbol context, latest price, absolute move, percentage move, freshness/session state.
- Quote row hydration reuses P0.4's listing-oriented market identity rule — not a watchlist-specific quote model.
- Add-to-watchlist from public subject routes uses the inline auth interrupt from P0.1 (preserves route + pending action across sign-in).
- Removing a member does not mutate the underlying subject, quote snapshot, or portfolio overlay state.

## Depends on

- P0.4 (reusable search entry + selected-subject handoff)
- P0.1 (inline auth interrupt contract)

## Downstream consumers

- P1.5 (portfolio & watchlist basics) — builds on this baseline
- P4.7 (dynamic watchlists & overlays) — extends baseline with derivation modes
- P5.1 (agent CRUD) — may target user-owned collections without inventing a separate system

## Considerations / gotchas

- Storing ticker strings instead of `SubjectRef`s seems easier but breaks on cross-listing and restatement. Always persist the resolved canonical id.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `app/watchlists/ManualWatchlist.tsx`
- `/v1/watchlists/*` handlers
- `watchlists`, `watchlist_members` tables (already in schema pack)

title: Implement default manual watchlist + membership CRUD
description: What: One implicit default manual watchlist per user. Add/remove/view members only. Why: Later watchlist concepts build on this baseline. Contract: Membership is canonical SubjectRef, idempotent at subject level. Verification: Adding same subject twice yields one row.

title: Implement watchlist row quote hydration (reuses P0.4 rule)
description: What: Row hydration pulls the same listing-oriented quote snapshot as P0.4 landing. Why: No second quote identity model for watchlist rows. Contract: Row shape display id + listing context + price + move + pct + freshness. Verification: Row and symbol-detail landing show identical values for same subject.

title: Implement add-to-watchlist with inline auth interrupt
description: What: From a public subject route, 'Add to watchlist' triggers the inline auth interrupt and resumes after sign-in. Why: Videos show this flow. Contract: Unauth click preserves route + pending add; post-sign-in completes. Verification: E2E test with unauth user.

title: P0.5 DB bootstrap (schema pack + migrations)
description:
# P0.5 — DB bootstrap (schema pack + migrations)

## What

Apply `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` against a PostgreSQL 15+ database. Set up a migration runner, seed scripts for reference data (metrics registry, source providers), enum registry, and a partition/index plan for the evidence plane (`facts`, `claims`, `snapshots`, `tool_call_logs`).

## Why

Every service and feature in P1..P6 assumes these tables exist. The schema encodes the evidence plane's truth-vs-evidence discipline (I1, I3) and the identity layer (issuer/instrument/listing). Getting the schema right here prevents ad-hoc table sprawl later.

## Contract / acceptance

- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` applies cleanly on a fresh Postgres 15+ database.
- Extension `pgcrypto` is installed (for `gen_random_uuid`).
- All enum types in the schema pack are created.
- Seed script populates `metrics` with the core metric registry (common ratios, accounting metrics).
- Migration runner supports forward/backward migrations and is used for every schema change after initial apply.
- Indexes in the schema pack are present (notably `facts_subject_metric_idx`, `facts_metric_period_idx`, `claims_document_idx`, `snapshots_created_idx`).
- Partition strategy documented for `facts` and `tool_call_logs` by time (monthly is a sensible default).

## Depends on

- None (foundation work).

## Downstream consumers

- All P0..P6 services depend on this.

## Considerations / gotchas

- D1 is not a fit for the evidence plane — current D1 limits are 10 GB per database on paid plans. Keep evidence plane on Postgres; D1 may carry app metadata if useful.
- `facts.supersedes` / `superseded_by` are self-referential — restatements form a linked chain. Don't break that by overwriting rows.
- `subject_kind + subject_id` polymorphic references are intentional (per-kind FK would explode the table count). Application code enforces referential integrity.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `spec/finance_research_db_schema.sql` (applied, not modified)
- `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` (wraps the schema pack)
- `db/seed/metrics.sql`
- `db/migrate.ts` (runner)

title: Apply finance_research_db_schema.sql to fresh Postgres
description: What: Run the normative schema pack. Why: Foundation for every downstream service. Contract: Schema applies cleanly on Postgres 15+ with pgcrypto. Verification: Migration runs; psql \dt lists all tables from the pack.

title: Implement migration runner + forward/backward migrations
description: What: Migration tool for every schema change after initial apply. Why: Ad-hoc DDL destroys reproducibility and blocks rollback. Contract: Each migration has up() and down(); runner tracks applied set. Verification: Roll forward then backward on a test DB.

title: Seed reference data (metrics registry, common sources)
description: What: Populate metrics with common definitions (revenue, EPS, margins, ratios) + minimal Source registry. Why: Fundamentals + market services reference metric_ids. Contract: Seed is idempotent. Verification: Re-running seed produces no duplicates.

title: Document partition strategy for facts and tool_call_logs
description: What: Decision doc + example DDL for monthly partitioning of facts and tool_call_logs. Why: Both tables grow unboundedly. Contract: Doc specifies partition key, retention policy, and cross-partition query pattern. Verification: Doc + sample partition in dev.

title: P0.6 Dev infra & observability skeleton
description:
# P0.6 — Dev infra & observability skeleton

## What

Bootstrap the development infrastructure: CI, test runner, lint/typecheck, local dev shell, feature-flag harness, plus a skeleton of the observability tables (`tool_call_logs`, `citation_logs`, `verifier_fail_logs`, `eval_run_results`) and a minimal SSE framework for chat streaming.

## Why

Without observability from day one, regressions in invariant-sensitive code paths are invisible. The spec explicitly names these log tables as day-one necessities — not afterthoughts — because evals drift is hard to detect without baselines.

## Contract / acceptance

- CI runs typecheck, lint, and tests on every PR.
- Local dev shell can run the full backend + frontend + postgres + redis with one command.
- Feature-flag harness exists (used for gating P6 features in dev).
- `tool_call_logs`, `citation_logs`, `verifier_fail_logs`, `eval_run_results` exist per schema pack and are writable.
- Golden-eval scaffolding exists (even if empty) so PX.1 can expand it.
- SSE route `/v1/chat/threads/:threadId/stream` exists as a stub (real wiring in P2.1).

## Depends on

- P0.5 (schema must be applied)

## Downstream consumers

- All phases depend on observability.
- PX.1 extends this skeleton with real evals and drift monitoring.
- P2.1 replaces the stub SSE route with the real streaming implementation.

## Considerations / gotchas

- Keep CI fast. Golden evals belong to a scheduled job, not every PR.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- `package.json` / `turbo.json` / `pnpm-workspace.yaml`
- `observability/logs/*`
- `evals/golden/*` (empty skeleton)

title: Set up CI (typecheck, lint, tests)
description: What: CI running typecheck, lint, tests on every PR. Why: Invariant-sensitive code needs baseline CI. Contract: CI must pass before merge. Verification: A trivial PR runs the full CI suite.

title: Bootstrap observability log tables + write helpers
description: What: tool_call_logs, citation_logs, verifier_fail_logs, eval_run_results writable from backend. Why: Day-one observability catches invariant drift. Contract: Every tool call writes tool_call_logs; every block citation writes citation_logs. Verification: Run any tool call; row appears.

title: Bootstrap SSE route stub for chat streaming
description: What: /v1/chat/threads/:threadId/stream route wired as SSE emitting stub events until P2.1. Why: Unblocks frontend streaming work during P0. Contract: Route accepts session; emits heartbeat. Verification: curl -N keeps connection open and emits heartbeats.

title: Epic P3 — Document & evidence plane
description:
# Epic P3 — Document & evidence plane

## Goal

Stand up the full document → claim → fact pipeline: source/document models with object storage for raw blobs, a reader extraction path that is the *only* code allowed to see raw untrusted text, the claim/event/impact graph with clustering, promotion rules for candidate facts, and the evidence-bundle assembler used by analyst-facing `get_evidence_bundle`.

## Child tasks

- P3.1 Source & document models + object storage (R2/S3) + content-hash dedupe
- P3.2 Document ingestion & reader extraction (SEC EDGAR, press, transcripts, news, X, Reddit, uploads)
- P3.3 Entity linking & mentions (reader-side subject linking)
- P3.4 Claim / event / impact graph & claim clustering (`ClaimCluster`, `ClaimClusterMember`)
- P3.5 Promotion rules for candidate facts → Fact with supersession
- P3.6 Evidence-bundle assembler (`build_evidence_bundle`, `get_evidence_bundle`)

## Exit criteria

- Analyst-facing code paths cannot read raw document bytes (enforced by tool audience).
- Documents with identical `content_hash` dedupe to one row.
- Claims carry `modality`, `attributed_to_type`, `effective_time`, `confidence`, `status`.
- `EntityImpact` edges route claims to subjects with `direction`, `channel`, `horizon`.
- Candidate facts from non-primary sources never silently become authoritative.
- A filing restatement supersedes the prior fact rather than overwriting it.
- `ClaimCluster` dedupes similar claims and powers Home-feed cluster dedupe.

## Invariants this epic primarily guards

- **I3** (documents are evidence, not truth)
- **I4** (analyst never sees raw untrusted text)
- **I6** (multi-entity reasoning via claims + impacts, not mention counts)

## Why this ordering

P4 (Home feed, Analyze with source categories, specialized social/news blocks) all assume the evidence plane exists. P5 (agents) runs reader extraction over deltas. Without P3 complete, agents have nothing to find, Home has nothing to rank, and Analyze templates cannot cite evidence.

title: P3.1 Source & document models + object storage
description:
# P3.1 — Source & document models + object storage

## What

The persistence layer for evidence ingestion: `Source`, `Document`, `Mention` tables already in the schema pack, plus R2/S3 object storage for raw blobs (referenced by `documents.raw_blob_id`), plus content-hash dedupe at the document level.

## Why

Raw document bytes belong outside the relational schema — they are large, immutable, and rarely queried after ingestion. Referencing them by blob id keeps the evidence graph small and makes deletion/retention straightforward. Content-hash dedupe at ingest prevents a single press release retrieved from multiple aggregators from producing multiple rows.

## Contract / acceptance

- Raw blobs land in object storage (R2/S3) keyed by `raw_blob_id` derived from content hash.
- `documents` rows reference `raw_blob_id` and carry `content_hash`, `parse_status`, `provider_doc_id`, `published_at`, `lang`.
- `sources` carry `trust_tier` (`primary | secondary | tertiary | user`) and `license_class`.
- Unique index on `(content_hash, raw_blob_id)` prevents duplicate document rows.
- `parent_document_id` supports threaded sources (Reddit threads, transcripts with segments).
- `deleted_at` supports soft-delete for retention/license compliance.
- Access to raw blobs is read-only; updates are never in-place (superseded documents get new rows).

## Depends on

- P0.5 (schema applied)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.2 (ingestion writes here)
- P3.3 (mentions reference documents)
- P3.4 (claims reference documents + sources)
- P3.6 (evidence bundles package documents)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Licensing: some provider content cannot be stored raw. Support a `license_class` that routes between blob storage and ephemeral fetch.
- Never expose raw blob URLs to analyst-audience tools. Reader tools only.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/ObjectStore.ts` (R2/S3 adapter)
- `services/evidence/DocumentRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/SourceRepo.ts`

title: P3.2 Document ingestion & reader extraction
description:
# P3.2 — Document ingestion & reader extraction

## What

The reader pipeline: acquisition from each source class (SEC filings, issuer press, earnings transcripts, news articles, research notes, X/tweets, Reddit posts/threads, user uploads), canonicalization, parsing, entity-linking, and extraction of claims / candidate-facts / events / impacts / sentiment via the reader-audience tools (`extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment`).

## Why

The reader boundary (I4) is the prompt-injection containment frontier. If raw untrusted text ever reaches the analyst, a malicious document can steer downstream tool calls. Keeping all raw-text handling inside reader-audience tools means analyst-audience code paths are statically incapable of being poisoned.

## Contract / acceptance

- Reader-only tools: `search_raw_documents`, `fetch_raw_document`, `extract_mentions`, `extract_claims`, `extract_candidate_facts`, `extract_events`, `classify_sentiment` (per `finance_research_tool_registry.json`).
- These tools are the ONLY code paths that may access raw blob bytes.
- Analyst-audience tools receive structured outputs: claims, events, candidate facts, authoritative facts, evidence bundles, entity impacts, claim clusters.
- Content-hash dedupe at ingest; `content_hash` identical → one `documents` row.
- Each ingestion writes: `documents` row, `mentions`, `claims` + `claim_arguments` + `entity_impacts`, `claim_evidence`, `events` + `event_subjects`, candidate facts.
- Per-source-class promotion rules are applied (see P3.5).
- Failed extractions below a confidence threshold queue into a reviewer queue (P6.3) rather than silently auto-promoting.
- Ingestion is idempotent: replaying the same source material does not duplicate rows.

## Depends on

- P3.1 (document/source models + object storage)
- PX.2 (reader-audience tool runtime — enforces the boundary)

## Relationship to P2.4 (snapshot assembler)

**P3.2 is NOT blocked by P2.4.** Extracted evidence (claims, facts, events) lands in the evidence plane independently. P2.4 *consumes* these records at snapshot-seal time — it does not gate extraction. The two can be built in parallel once P3.1 + PX.2 exist.

## Downstream consumers

- P3.3, P3.4, P3.5 (downstream extraction/clustering/promotion)
- P4.6 (specialized social/news blocks read extracted claims)
- P5.2 (agent runs rerun ingestion over deltas)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Prompt-injection containment: tests must verify that no reader-extracted text can steer analyst behavior.
- SEC EDGAR rate-limit rules apply: respect User-Agent policy and request rate.
- Reddit/X content may have restrictive licensing — store metadata + canonical URL, not always the full body.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/ingest/sec/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/news/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/social/*`
- `services/evidence/ingest/upload/*`
- `services/evidence/reader/*` (extraction tools)

title: P3.3 Entity linking & mentions
description:
# P3.3 — Entity linking & mentions

## What

Reader-side entity linking that produces `Mention` rows: for every document, identify the subjects (issuers, instruments, listings, themes, macro topics) mentioned, their `prominence` (headline, lead, body, incidental), `mention_count`, and `confidence`.

## Why

A single news article about "Apple's Q3 results" may mention Apple (issuer), AAPL (listing), AAPL options chain (instrument), chips supply theme (theme), and semiconductors (macro topic). Mentions are how reader output fans out to the right set of subjects for downstream routing, but mentions alone are NOT evidence of impact — that's what `EntityImpact` is for (I6).

## Contract / acceptance

- Every ingested `Document` produces zero or more `Mention` rows.
- `mentions.prominence` is one of `headline | lead | body | incidental` — prominence affects later ranking (headline mentions weigh more for Home feed).
- `mentions.confidence` is reader's self-reported entity-linking confidence.
- Mentions reference `subject_kind + subject_id` polymorphically.
- Mentions do NOT imply entity impact — a document can mention a subject incidentally without affecting it. Routing and alerting use `EntityImpact`, not `Mention`.

## Depends on

- P3.1 (documents table)
- P0.3 (resolver — entity linking consults canonical identity)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.4 (claims reference documents; arguments + impacts layer on top of mentions)
- P4.4 (Home feed ranks by mention prominence + severity + recency)
- P4.6 (mention_volume block reads aggregated mentions)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT use mention counts as a proxy for entity impact. That's a deliberate non-goal called out in the spec.
- Entity linking may be ambiguous — preserve ranked candidates like the resolver does, rather than silently picking.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/reader/EntityLinker.ts`
- `services/evidence/reader/extract_mentions.ts` (tool wrapper)

title: P3.4 Claim / event / impact graph & claim clustering
description:
# P3.4 — Claim / event / impact graph & claim clustering

## What

The core reasoning graph: `Claim`, `ClaimArgument`, `EntityImpact`, `ClaimEvidence`, `Event`, `EventSubject`, plus `ClaimCluster` / `ClaimClusterMember` for deduping similar claims. A document can fan out into multiple claims, each with different subject roles and impacts. Events aggregate claims and source refs into state-change records.

## Why

The reasoning unit is the claim plus its arguments and impacts — not the document. This is invariant I6: a news article about a regulator fining a supplier can produce one claim with arguments `{regulator: subject, supplier: object, manufacturer: affected_party}` and impacts `{supplier: negative/regulation, manufacturer: negative/supply_chain}`. Clustering dedupes claims across sources so the Home feed shows one card for a story, not three.

## Contract / acceptance

- `Claim` carries `predicate`, `text_canonical`, `polarity`, `modality` (`asserted | estimated | speculative | rumored | quoted`), `attributed_to_type`, `effective_time`, `confidence`, `status` (`extracted | corroborated | disputed | rejected`).
- `ClaimArgument` binds claim to subjects with `role` (`subject | object | customer | supplier | competitor | regulator | beneficiary | constrained_party | affected_party`).
- `EntityImpact` routes claims to subjects with `direction` (`positive | negative | mixed | unknown`), `channel` (`demand | pricing | supply_chain | regulation | competition | balance_sheet | sentiment`), `horizon` (`near_term | medium_term | long_term`).
- `Event` has `event_type` (`earnings_release | guidance_update | rating_change | m_and_a | split | dividend | product_launch | lawsuit | macro_event | theme_event`) + `status` + `source_claim_ids` + `source_ids`.
- `ClaimCluster.canonical_signature` is unique; similar claims across sources join a cluster via `ClaimClusterMember` with `relation: support | contradict`.
- Cluster has `support_count`, `contradiction_count`, `aggregate_confidence` for ranking.

## Depends on

- P3.2 (claims/events come from reader extraction)
- P3.3 (claims arguments reference subjects via mentions)

## Downstream consumers

- P3.5 (promotion rules consult modality + source class)
- P3.6 (evidence bundles package claims + supporting documents)
- P4.4 (Home feed dedupes on ClaimCluster)
- P5.3 (findings reference claim_cluster_ids)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Canonical signature design is critical for cluster quality. Don't hash only the text — include subject set + event type + effective time.
- Impact routing drives alerting. A claim about a supplier should route impacts to the manufacturer by supply-chain channel.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/graph/ClaimRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/graph/EventRepo.ts`
- `services/evidence/graph/Clusterer.ts`

title: P3.5 Promotion rules for candidate facts
description:
# P3.5 — Promotion rules for candidate facts

## What

Deterministic rules that govern when an extracted candidate fact becomes an authoritative `Fact`. Inputs: source class (filing / press / transcript / news / social / upload), extraction confidence, corroboration by other sources, issuer self-attestation, `Metric` definition. Outputs: a new `Fact` row with `verification_status` (`authoritative | corroborated | candidate | disputed`), or a candidate fact that stays pending.

## Why

Without explicit promotion rules, every extracted number races to become truth. The spec's per-source-class promotion discipline (I3) is how documents stay "evidence not truth."

## Contract / acceptance

Promotion rules by source class (lifted from spec):

| Document kind | Typical outputs | Authoritative fact creation |
|---|---|---|
| Filing / primary issuer disclosure | facts, claims, events | Yes |
| Press release / earnings release | facts, claims, events | Yes, under parser rules |
| Transcript | claims, events, candidate facts | Sometimes, with attribution |
| News article / research note | claims, events, candidate facts, impacts | Usually no |
| Tweet / X / Reddit | claims, sentiment, impact leads | No |
| User upload / memo | user-scoped claims, notes, optional candidate facts | User-scoped only unless validated |

- Normalized statement values from P1.2 land as `Fact` rows when authoritative, as `Computation` rows when derived.
- Facts are immutable except through supersession. Restatements use `facts.supersedes` / `superseded_by` — NEVER overwrite.
- `invalidated_at` marks a fact as no longer applicable without supersession.
- Low-confidence extractions route to reviewer queue (P6.3) rather than auto-promoting.

## Depends on

- P1.2 (normalized statement layer — the canonical value plane promotion targets)
- P3.2 (candidate fact extraction)
- P3.4 (claim corroboration context)

## Downstream consumers

- P1.2b (aggregations read authoritative facts)
- P2.4 (snapshot manifests bind to facts with known verification_status)
- P5.3 (findings reference promoted facts for severity scoring)
- P6.3 (reviewer queue consumes low-confidence rejects)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Do NOT use `facts.scale` as a workaround for unit mismatches — normalize before writing.
- Preserve `definition_version` — if a metric definition changes, old and new values must be distinguishable.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/promotion/Rules.ts`
- `services/evidence/promotion/Supersession.ts`

title: P3.6 Evidence-bundle assembler
description:
# P3.6 — Evidence-bundle assembler

## What

The service that builds `EvidenceBundle` objects on request: given a set of `claim_ids` or `event_ids`, return the canonical set of supporting documents, evidence locators (`claim_evidence.locator`), and evidence excerpts needed to justify a claim. Implements analyst-facing `build_evidence_bundle` and `get_evidence_bundle` tools.

## Why

The analyst never sees raw document bodies (I4). When it needs to justify a claim in its response (e.g., "revenue grew 12% YoY driven by Services, per the Q3 10-Q"), it calls `get_evidence_bundle` and receives structured evidence with locators and excerpt hashes — not raw text.

## Contract / acceptance

- `build_evidence_bundle({claim_ids?, event_ids?})` returns `{bundle_id, documents[], evidence[]}`.
- Evidence entries carry `{claim_id, document_id, locator (json), excerpt_hash, confidence}`.
- Documents in the bundle expose metadata (`title`, `author`, `published_at`, `canonical_url`, `source.trust_tier`) but NOT raw body.
- Bundles are immutable — a bundle_id always resolves to the same content.
- The analyst-facing response can cite `bundle_id` in block `source_refs[]` and RichText `ref_kind: claim` segments.

## Depends on

- P3.4 (claims + events exist)

## Downstream consumers

- Analyst tool `get_evidence_bundle` (PX.2)
- P4.2 (Analyze memos build bundles for source sections)
- P4.6 (specialized news/social blocks cite bundles)

## Considerations / gotchas

- Excerpt hashes (not raw excerpts) go through analyst-audience tools — this preserves I4 while letting the frontend fetch verified excerpts via a separate reader-audience endpoint.

## Files to touch (likely)

- `services/evidence/bundle/BundleAssembler.ts`
- `/v1/evidence/evidence-bundles` handler

title: Implement agent loop (delta ingest -> extract -> cluster -> analyst -> emit)
